title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Mirage of Truth: Information in the Post-Fact Era
content.subtitle: Analyzing the erosion of objective reality in the digital age.
content.text: The term 'Post-Truth' was named the word of the year in 2016, but the phenomenon it describes—the elevation of emotion over objective fact—has only intensified since then. In theory, the internet was supposed to be the ultimate democratic library, providing every citizen with access to the sum of human knowledge. [31] In practice, however, the digital landscape has fractured into a thousand 'echo chambers,' where users are fed information that confirms their existing biases rather than challenging them. [/31] The result is not an informed public, but a deeply polarized one. || The mechanism behind this fragmentation is the algorithm. Designed to maximize 'engagement,' social media platforms prioritize content that triggers a strong emotional response, particularly outrage or fear. [32] Because factual nuance is rarely as exciting as a sensationalist headline, truth is often the first casualty of the attention economy. [/32] We are no longer presented with a shared set of facts upon which to debate; we are presented with different versions of reality altogether. || [33] This erosion of objectivity is exacerbated by the decline of 'gatekeeper' institutions—traditional news organizations that once served to verify information before it reached the public. [/33] While the democratization of information has empowered marginalized voices, it has also removed the filters that protected society from misinformation and propaganda. When everyone is a 'journalist,' the distinction between a researched report and an unfounded conspiracy theory becomes dangerously blurred. || [34] Some psychologists argue that this is not a new problem, but a digital amplification of a prehistoric trait: 'tribalism.' [/34] Humans are biologically wired to value the approval of their group over the accuracy of an abstract fact. In the past, this tribalism was limited by geography; today, it is global and instantaneous. We find our 'tribe' online, and anyone outside of it is viewed not just as wrong, but as an existential enemy. This makes genuine dialogue almost impossible. || [35] To combat this, some tech companies are introducing 'fact-checking' labels and tweaking their algorithms to promote authoritative sources. [/35] While helpful, these measures often backfire. For a user deep within an echo chamber, a 'fact-check' label is often seen as evidence of a conspiracy by the 'establishment' to silence the truth. The more an individual is corrected, the more they may retreat into their defensive position. || [36] Ultimately, the solution to the post-truth crisis may not be technological, but educational. [/36] We must cultivate 'digital literacy'—the ability to critically evaluate the source, intent, and bias of every piece of information we consume. It requires a fundamental shift in our relationship with the screen, moving from passive consumption to active, skeptical inquiry. In an age where truth is a choice, we must be taught how to choose wisely.

questions.question: What does the author suggest about the internet's original purpose versus its current reality?
options: A) It has created divided groups that only see information they already agree with.
B) It has made people more open-minded by exposing them to many cultures.
C) It has replaced traditional libraries as the most trusted source of facts.
D) It has succeeded in becoming a library that everyone can use for free.
questions.explanation: The text explains that instead of a 'democratic library,' the internet has fractured into 'echo chambers' where users are fed information that 'confirms their existing biases.'

questions.question: According to the second paragraph, how does the 'attention economy' affect the truth?
options: A) It ensures that every user has an equal chance of being heard.
B) It allows people to find the truth more quickly through sensational headlines.
C) It makes detailed, nuanced information less likely to be shared.
D) It encourages platforms to hire more factual journalists.
questions.explanation: The author says 'factual nuance is rarely as exciting' as sensationalism, so truth is sacrificed for 'engagement.'

questions.question: What is the consequence of the decline of 'gatekeeper' institutions?
options: A) Traditional news organizations have become more powerful than ever.
B) People are no longer interested in hearing from marginalized voices.
C) It has become harder for the public to distinguish between facts and lies.
D) Journalists are now required to be more accurate to survive.
questions.explanation: The text states that removing filters (gatekeepers) means the distinction between a researched report and a conspiracy theory is 'dangerously blurred.'

questions.question: The author mentions 'tribalism' to explain why...
options: A) it is easier to talk to people who have different opinions.
B) humans are naturally better at logical thinking than animals.
C) digital communication has made us more interested in geography.
D) we prioritize group loyalty over objective reality.
questions.explanation: The text says humans are 'wired to value the approval of their group over the accuracy of an abstract fact.'

questions.question: Why might 'fact-checking' labels be ineffective according to the author?
options: A) Because the algorithms often forget to apply them to popular posts.
B) Because most users don't know how to read the labels.
C) Because they are too expensive for tech companies to implement.
D) Because they can be interpreted as a sign of bias by those being corrected.
questions.explanation: The author states that for some, the label is seen as evidence of a 'conspiracy' to silence them, making them retreat further into their views.

questions.question: What is the author's primary recommendation for solving the 'post-truth' crisis?
options: A) Training individuals to be more critical and skeptical of online content.
B) Returning to a time when only a few traditional news outlets were allowed.
C) Creating more advanced AI to identify misinformation automatically.
D) Banning all social media algorithms that promote outrage.
questions.explanation: The text says the solution is 'educational' and involves cultivating 'digital literacy' and 'active, skeptical inquiry.'
